*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: .sisyphus/drafts/ocx-portable-profile.md
+# Draft: OCX Portable Profile
+
+## Requirements (confirmed)
+- [goal]: изучить текущий setup пользователя и подготовить план по превращению его в переносимый OCX-профиль для развёртывания на другой машине
+- [must include]: `opencode/oh-my-openagent.jsonc`
+- [must include]: `opencode/oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc`
+- [must include]: `opencode/omo-switch`
+- [must include]: нужные `opencode/skills/*`, `opencode/agents/*`, `opencode/command/*`
+- [must exclude]: `opencode/profiles/default/*`
+- [must exclude]: `opencode/antigravity-accounts.json`
+- [must exclude]: `opencode/local-plugins`
+- [runtime assumption]: внешние CLI вроде `fzf`, `workmux`, `tmux`, `rtk` будут на целевой машине; это нужно описать в README, а не исключать из профиля
+
+## Technical Decisions
+- [profile model]: будущий результат — отдельный переносимый OCX profile bundle, а не копия bootstrap-слоя после `ocx init --global`
+- [config strategy]: набор переключаемых `oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc` считается first-class частью профиля, а не вспомогательными файлами
+- [switching UX]: `omo-switch` остаётся частью профиля как стандартный способ переключения активного orchestration-конфига
+- [ocx workflow]: основная работа по созданию/установке/запуску профиля должна опираться на CLI-команды OCX
+
+## Research Findings
+- [current setup]: `opencode/opencode.jsonc` — основной OpenCode config c plugins/providers/MCP
+- [current setup]: `opencode/oh-my-openagent.jsonc` — активная orchestration-конфигурация
+- [current setup]: `opencode/omo-switch` переключает активный `oh-my-openagent.jsonc` через выбор среди `oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc` с помощью `fzf`
+- [portability]: `profiles/default/*` — bootstrap scaffold, созданный через `ocx init --global`, не должен входить в будущий профиль
+- [portability]: machine-local state (`antigravity-accounts.json`, localhost/app-specific configs) не входит в переносимый bundle
+
+## Open Questions
+- [artifact shape]: в какой именно форме хранить профиль в репо относительно OCX registry/profile conventions
+- [documentation scope]: какой README/инструкции достаточно для runtime dependencies и install flow
+
+## Scope Boundaries
+- INCLUDE: проектирование одного переносимого OCX-профиля для текущего setup
+- INCLUDE: упаковку конфигов, preset-файлов, switch-script, нужных skills/agents/commands
+- INCLUDE: шаги CLI OCX для создания, наполнения, установки и проверки профиля
+- EXCLUDE: перенос `profiles/default/*`
+- EXCLUDE: перенос `local-plugins`
+- EXCLUDE: перенос auth/account state и прочих machine-local runtime данных
*** End Patch

content.summary:
User initially requested in Russian: "изучи https://ocx.kdco.dev/docs/ создай контекст, мне нужна помощь ulw" under an injected `<ultrawork-mode>` instruction set that mandated starting responses with `ULTRAWORK MODE ENABLED!`. I treated the task as research/context creation, not implementation.

I launched background research on official OCX docs plus local ULW references. Direct fetch of the docs root failed once due to a socket close, so I relied on targeted webfetches plus background librarian/explore tasks instead of re-searching the same topics manually.

User then pasted substantial OCX reference content from the project docs/README covering: profiles, registries, SHA-verification, installation, quick start, `ocx init --global`, `ocx profile add ws --source ... --from ... --global`, `ocx oc -p ws`, migration commands (`ocx migrate`, `--apply`, `--global`), component installation, and the shadcn-like philosophy that OCX copies components into `.opencode/`. I used this pasted content as additional context for later synthesis.

Parallel direct research on the local dotfiles repo found many `ulw`, `ultrawork`, `ultrawork-mode`, and `ulw-loop` matches, primarily in `opencode/schema.json` and archived prompt/session files under `.entire/metadata/.../prompt.txt`. Important local findings from direct grep/read:
- `opencode/schema.json` contains `"ultrawork"` schema sections and includes `"ulw-loop"` in command-related definitions.
- `opencode/AGENTS.md` contains local OpenCode rules about model filtering, specifically using `models.whitelist` / `models.blacklist` in `opencode/opencode.jsonc`, with the explicit warning that unknown keys are silently ignored and plugin-specific options do not globally filter models.
- Later direct reads of `opencode/schema.json` near the `ultrawork` and `ralph_loop` / `background_task` sections confirmed these are formal schema concepts in the local oh-my-openagent/open opencode setup.

I summarized an initial working model for the user: OCX is the launcher/config layer; ULW is not an OCX CLI command but an agent behavior/orchestration layer inside the local OpenCode/oh-my-opencode setup. I also noted that profile visibility filtering (`exclude/include`) is likely relevant to whether ULW “sees” the right instructions and files.

User then pasted additional OCX guide content for **Oh-My-OpenCode Setup** (`omo` profile). Key details from that user-provided content:
- `ocx profile add omo --source kit/omo --from https://ocx-kit.kdco.dev --global`
- `ocx oc -p omo`
- The `omo` profile includes `opencode.jsonc`, `oh-my-opencode.json`, `ocx.jsonc`, `AGENTS.md`
- It is preconfigured with free OpenCode Zen models and is intended as a portable AI coding environment.
I updated the mental model accordingly: **OCX profile selects environment, oh-my-opencode provides agent system, ULW belongs to that agent behavior layer**.

User then asked to inspect `docs/cli` “in the repo.” Local glob over `/Users/tim/dev/dotfiles/**/docs/cli/**/*` found no such directory in the current dotfiles repo, so I inferred they meant the upstream `kdcokenny/ocx` repository. I launched a background librarian task specifically against that upstream repository.

Background task results and direct webfetchs produced the following synthesized OCX documentation map:
- Profiles Overview page established that profiles live under `~/.config/opencode/profiles/<name>/` and contain `ocx.jsonc`, `opencode.jsonc`, and `AGENTS.md`.
- Profile resolution order: local config `profile` field → `-p/--profile` → `OCX_PROFILE` → `default` → none.
- CLI Reference page established OCX command groups, including `init`, `add`, `remove`, `update`, `search`, `verify`, `registry`, `build`, `self`, `profile`, `config`, `opencode`, plus global flags and exit codes.
- `ocx opencode` is the formal command; examples and docs also use the alias `ocx oc`.
- Official docs index did not expose a dedicated `migrate` page even though user-pasted material mentioned `ocx migrate`.

The upstream `kdcokenny/ocx` repo inspection task returned a precise `docs/cli` file map from commit `45c1faed57abb87f862dc5d01ecf17945a0c57f8`:
- `commands.mdx, init.mdx, add.mdx, remove.mdx, update.mdx, search.mdx, verify.mdx, registry.mdx, build.mdx, self.mdx, profile.mdx, config.mdx, opencode.mdx`
- **No `docs/cli/migrate.mdx` exists**.
It also found:
- `docs/cli/opencode.mdx` documents `ocx opencode` / alias `ocx oc`, with only OCX-specific flags `-p/--profile` and `--no-rename`, passing everything else through to OpenCode.
- `docs/cli/profile.mdx` and source confirm `ocx profile` / `ocx p` subcommands are `list`, `add`, `remove`, `move`, `show`.
- Profile config editing is done through `ocx config edit -p <profile>` rather than a separate `profile config` command.
- `ocx verify` has a real docs page; `ocx migrate` exists in source and bootstrap registration, defaults to dry-run, but lacks a `docs/cli` page.
- There is a separate maintainer migration note for an older ghost-mode → profile-system migration, distinct from `ocx migrate`.

A background librarian task focused on the `omo` profile and oh-my-opencode relationship added these concrete findings:
- The `omo` profile in OCX is conceptually made of `opencode.jsonc`, `oh-my-opencode.json` / compatible naming, `ocx.jsonc`, and `AGENTS.md`.
- The OCX guide says `omo` is installed from `kit/omo` and launched with `ocx oc -p omo`.
- In the OCX repo, the `omo` profile package wires in free-model defaults and the oh-my-opencode plugin.
- For oh-my-opencode, configuration docs recognize both legacy `oh-my-opencode.json[c]` and renamed `oh-my-openagent.json[c]` naming.
- oh-my-opencode docs explicitly document `ulw` / `ultrawork` as an ultrawork-mode trigger and `/ulw-loop` as a related loop command. This supported the conclusion that ULW belongs to the agent/orchestration layer, not OCX itself.

Another background explore task focused on local ULW context across the current codebase/config returned these key findings:
- `opencode/schema.json` formally defines `ultrawork`, `ralph_loop`, `background_task`, and related configuration surfaces.
- Canonical behavior examples for ultrawork are present mostly in archived prompt files under `.entire/metadata/.../prompt.txt`, including requirements such as beginning responses with `ULTRAWORK MODE ENABLED!`, doing broad exploration first, delegating to specialist/background agents, and performing strong verification/manual QA.
- It characterized ULW as “Ultrawork Mode”: a high-autonomy OpenCode/Oh My OpenAgent workflow. Trigger text is `ulw` or `ultrawork`, and `ulw-loop` exists as a schema/command concept.
- It connected `ralph_loop` to persistence/continue-until-complete behavior.
- It concluded that OCX itself does not define ULW behavior; the behavior lives in the prompt/config/orchestration layer.

After all research finished, I provided the user with a synthesized, stable mental model:
- **OCX** = launcher/config/profile/visibility layer.
- **`omo` profile** = a ready-made OCX profile that boots OpenCode with oh-my-opencode wired in.
- **ULW** = an oh-my-opencode / Sisyphus orchestration mode, triggered inside that environment (`ulw` / `ultrawork`), not an OCX CLI command.
- Therefore the operational chain is: `ocx oc -p omo` (or another profile) → profile loads OpenCode + oh-my-opencode config → within that agent stack the user invokes ULW behavior.
- Another important insight I shared: visibility control in OCX profiles (`exclude/include`) is likely the first thing to inspect if ULW seems not to see the right instructions/files in a repo.

User then shifted from generic OCX/ULW understanding to a concrete personal setup migration problem. They explained that before discovering OCX they already had a detailed working setup: OmyOpenAgent/Oh My OpenAgent plus plugins and skills, with some plugins declared via config and some local. They asked me to study their current setup to determine whether it can be turned into a portable OCX profile that could be deployed on another machine.

In response, I launched new background explore tasks to:
1. map the current local OpenCode/oh-my-openagent setup in the dotfiles repo,
2. assess which parts are portable as an OCX profile vs machine-local or requiring adaptation.
I also did direct local exploration over `/Users/tim/dev/dotfiles/opencode`.

Direct local exploration found and read the core setup files:
- `opencode/opencode.jsonc`: main OpenCode config with plugin array including `oh-my-openagent@3.15.0`, auth plugins, plannotator, DCP, Wakatime, Push, etc.; default model `openai/gpt-5.2`; remote MCPProxy at `http://localhost:8080/mcp/`; `enabled_providers`; markdown LSP command using `npx -y remark-language-server --stdio`; detailed provider model whitelist/variants. It also contained a **commented** local plugin entry: `file:///Users/tim/dev/dotfiles/opencode/local-plugins`.
- `opencode/oh-my-openagent.jsonc`: active orchestration config defining agents (`sisyphus`, `atlas`, `oracle`, `librarian`, `explore`, `hephaestus`, `sisyphus-junior`, `multimodal-looker`, `plan`, `OpenCode-Builder`, `prometheus`, `metis`, `momus`), categories, `sisyphus_agent` settings, `ralph_loop`, `background_task` concurrency limits, experimental truncation/auto-resume settings, and disabled skills (`playwright`). No absolute host paths were in this file.
- `opencode/profiles/default/ocx.jsonc`: OCX profile scaffold created from `ocx init --global`, with generic excludes for `CLAUDE.md`, `CONTEXT.md`, `.opencode/**`, `opencode.jsonc`, and `opencode.json`, plus empty `include`.
- `opencode/profiles/default/opencode.jsonc`: empty `{}`.
- `opencode/profiles/default/AGENTS.md`: boilerplate comment showing it is included by `ocx opencode -p default`.
- `opencode/omo-switch`: bash script using `fzf` to enumerate `oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc` in the directory and update `oh-my-openagent.jsonc` symlink to the chosen preset.
- `opencode/local-plugins/package.json`: local plugin package `opencode-antigravity-auth` v1.6.0, Node >=20, repository `NoeFabris/opencode-antigravity-auth`, build/test scripts, and dependency on `@opencode-ai/plugin`.
- `opencode/local-plugins/README.md`: lengthy installation/usage docs for Antigravity/Gemini OAuth plugin, explicitly referring to `~/.config/opencode/opencode.json`, account state files, auth login, and machine-local runtime state.
- `opencode/schema.json`: local oh-my-openagent schema including `ralph_loop` and `background_task` formal definitions.
- `opencode/plugins/*`: local plugin files `traces.ts`, `workmux-status.ts`, `rtk-rewrite.ts`, `engram.ts`.
- `opencode/skills/*`, `opencode/agents/*`, `opencode/command/*`: locally curated skill packs, agents, and command shims.

The first setup-mapping background task returned a structured inventory showing the setup is split across layers:
- primary OpenCode config (`opencode/opencode.jsonc` and a separate minimal `opencode.json` elsewhere in repo context),
- active orchestration config (`opencode/oh-my-openagent.jsonc`),
- many preset variants (`oh-my-openagent_openai.jsonc`, `..._openai-google-hybrid.jsonc`, `..._gemini-only.jsonc`, `..._gemini-flash-only.jsonc`, `..._antigravity-opus_4_6.jsonc`, `..._antigravity-flash_only.jsonc`, `..._omniroute.jsonc`, `..._omniroute-raw.jsonc`, `..._glm.jsonc`, `..._opencode-go.jsonc`, plus backup/generated files),
- `omo-switch` as the preset switcher,
- `profiles/default/*` as an OCX profile scaffold,
- local `skills/`, `agents/`, and `command/` directories,
- launchd-related files and some machine-local runtime data.
It explicitly concluded that the detailed behavior currently lives outside the default OCX scaffold in repo-level config and preset files.

While synthesizing this, the user clarified an important scope change: **`local-plugins` is not required**, but the **switchable `oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc` configs and the switching script must be part of the included portable set**. I acknowledged this and updated the working model: the portable artifact should treat the preset family and `omo-switch` as first-class profile content, not as incidental files. I also noted that `omo-switch` implies `fzf` must exist on target machines.

The final portability-assessment background task then categorized files roughly into:
- **Likely portable as-is**: `opencode/oh-my-openagent.jsonc`, `opencode/ocx.jsonc`, `opencode/profiles/default/ocx.jsonc` scaffold (from a pure portability standpoint), `profiles/default/opencode.jsonc`, `profiles/default/AGENTS.md`, generic agents/skills like `agents/rubber-duck.md`, `skills/open-pr/SKILL.md`, `skills/find-skills/SKILL.md`.
- **Portable with edits/runtime assumptions**: `opencode/opencode.jsonc` (because of env-backed provider config, localhost MCP proxy, and commented absolute local-plugin path), `opencode.json`, `local-plugins` docs/package, `skills/workmux`, `skills/worktree`, `skills/merge`, `skills/rtk-compress`, `skills/plannotator-compound` due to extra CLI/home-dir assumptions.
- **Likely non-portable**: `opencode/opencode_mcps.json` with localhost and app-bundle path assumptions, `opencode/com.opencode.serve.plist` with hard-coded `/Users/tim` and Homebrew paths.
It concluded that the setup is mostly portable at the declarative layer but not fully distributable as-is without separating out environment/machine-specific pieces.

I then gave the user a concise bullet summary saying, in essence:
- yes, this can become an OCX profile, but not as a full machine snapshot;
- future profile should include `opencode/oh-my-openagent.jsonc`, all `oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc`, `opencode/omo-switch`, plus needed `skills/`, `agents/`, and `command/` content;
- I initially also mentioned `opencode/profiles/default/*` as part of the transferable scaffold.

The user corrected that assumption sharply and provided exact intent that must be preserved:
- `profiles/default/ocx.jsonc` and more broadly `profiles/default/*` **must not be part of the future portable profile**, because they are just the base/default configuration created by `ocx init --global` — the bootstrap foundation into which new profiles appear.
- `antigravity-accounts.json` and `local-plugins` are indeed not needed.
- Files I had classified as “portable with adaptation” are actually to be treated as portable because the target machine will definitely have the relevant CLIs; these requirements should simply be documented in README.
- The user explicitly told me to keep all OCX CLI commands in mind because most of the upcoming work will be performed using the OCX CLI.

I incorporated that correction and restated the revised mental model:
- do **not** include `opencode/profiles/default/*` in the future profile,
- future profile core should be `opencode/opencode.jsonc`, `opencode/oh-my-openagent.jsonc`, all preset `opencode/oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc`, `opencode/omo-switch`, and selected `opencode/skills/*`, `opencode/agents/*`, `opencode/command/*`,
- `antigravity-accounts.json` and `local-plugins` are excluded,
- external CLI dependencies like `fzf`, `workmux`, `tmux`, `rtk` are assumed to exist on target machines and should be documented rather than treated as blockers,
- `default` OCX profile scaffold is merely bootstrap output from `ocx init --global`, not the artifact we are trying to package,
- most future implementation work should use OCX CLI operations.

At the end of this compressed range, the user explicitly said: “Ну тогда составляй план и давай мне на ревью. Ты же сейчас в режиме планирования.” This is the current transition point into formal plan generation. No implementation has been done yet. The latest stable requirements before plan generation are:
- build a **single plan** for turning the current setup into a portable OCX profile,
- exclude `profiles/default/*`, `local-plugins`, and `antigravity-accounts.json`,
- include `opencode/opencode.jsonc`, `opencode/oh-my-openagent.jsonc`, the full set of `opencode/oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc`, `opencode/omo-switch`, and relevant `skills/`, `agents/`, `command/` content,
- treat runtime CLIs as guaranteed/pre-documented dependencies,
- plan should be based around OCX CLI workflow,
- user wants the plan for review now.

+## Context
+### Original Request
+Пользователь хочет превратить свой текущий детально настроенный setup OpenCode / oh-my-openagent в переносимый OCX-профиль, который можно взять на другой машине и развернуть. Пользователь отдельно уточнил, что `profiles/default/*` — это bootstrap-слой после `ocx init --global`, а не часть будущего профиля. Пользователь также уточнил, что `local-plugins` и `antigravity-accounts.json` не входят в профиль, а runtime CLI вроде `fzf`, `workmux`, `tmux`, `rtk` гарантированно будут на целевой машине и должны быть описаны в README.
+
+### Interview Summary
+- В профиль обязательно должны войти:
+  - `opencode/opencode.jsonc`
+  - `opencode/oh-my-openagent.jsonc`
+  - все `opencode/oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc`
+  - `opencode/omo-switch`
+  - нужные `opencode/skills/*`, `opencode/agents/*`, `opencode/command/*`
+- В профиль не должны входить:
+  - `opencode/profiles/default/*`
+  - `opencode/local-plugins`
+  - `opencode/antigravity-accounts.json`
+- Большая часть реализации должна использовать CLI-команды OCX.
+- Переключаемые preset-конфиги — это не вспомогательные файлы, а first-class часть переносимого профиля.
+
+### Metis Review (gaps addressed)
+- **Неоднозначность формы артефакта**: зафиксировано решение — один канонический профиль, внутри которого хранятся несколько переключаемых `oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc` preset-вариантов.
+- **Статус `omo-switch`**: зафиксировано решение — это обязательная часть UX профиля, а не опциональный helper.
+- **Главный acceptance gate**: зафиксировано решение — обязателен smoke test на чистой среде / чистом профиле после установки.
+- **Риск scope creep**: исключены `profiles/default/*`, `local-plugins`, auth/account state и machine-local служебные файлы.
+
+## Work Objectives
+### Core Objective
+Создать decision-complete путь упаковки текущего setup пользователя в переносимый OCX-профиль, который устанавливается и запускается через OCX CLI на другой машине, сохраняя привычный набор orchestration-конфигов и механизм их переключения.
+
+### Deliverables
+- Новый профиль OCX в отдельной profile-структуре, не завязанный на `profiles/default/*`
+- Перенесённый OpenCode config layer (`opencode.jsonc`)
+- Перенесённый orchestration layer (`oh-my-openagent.jsonc` + `oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc`)
+- Перенесённый selector-script `omo-switch`
+- Перенесённые нужные локальные `skills/`, `agents/`, `command/`
+- README с prerequisites, install flow, switch flow, verify flow, known exclusions
+- Smoke-test сценарий для проверки профиля на другой машине / в чистой среде
+
+### Definition of Done (verifiable conditions with commands)
+- Профиль создаётся через OCX CLI и появляется в списке `ocx profile list --global`
+- Установленный профиль содержит ожидаемый набор файлов и не содержит запрещённые файлы
+- `ocx oc -p <profile>` запускает OpenCode с профилем без обращения к `profiles/default/*`
+- `omo-switch` видит preset-файлы `oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc` и переключает активный `oh-my-openagent.jsonc`
+- README покрывает prerequisites, install, switch, verify, exclusions
+- Smoke test на чистой среде проходит по documented flow
+
+### Must Have
+- Один новый канонический профиль, отдельный от bootstrap-default profile
+- Явное правило включения preset-файлов `oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc`
+- Явное правило включения `omo-switch`
+- Явное правило исключения `profiles/default/*`, `local-plugins`, `antigravity-accounts.json`
+- Использование OCX CLI как основной operational surface
+- README с документированными runtime CLI dependency assumptions
+
+### Must NOT Have (guardrails, AI slop patterns, scope boundaries)
+- Не использовать `profiles/default/*` как базу для содержимого будущего профиля
+- Не переносить account/auth state, localhost-specific machine state и launchd/plist-службы
+- Не добавлять новые плагины, новые preset-конфиги или новые skills сверх уже существующего scope
+- Не пытаться тащить `local-plugins` в профиль
+- Не опираться на ручные неформализованные шаги, если их можно выразить через OCX CLI или явные copy/install steps
+

content.summary:
The conversation shifted from research into explicit planning. User said: "Ну тогда составляй план и давай мне на ревью. Ты же сейчас в режиме планирования." I treated this as the formal trigger to generate a plan rather than implement.

I announced I would fix the scope into a plan for packaging the current setup into a portable OCX profile bundle: include `opencode/opencode.jsonc`, `opencode/oh-my-openagent.jsonc`, all `opencode/oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc`, `opencode/omo-switch`, and needed `opencode/skills/*`, `opencode/agents/*`, `opencode/command/*`; exclude `opencode/profiles/default/*`, `opencode/local-plugins`, and `opencode/antigravity-accounts.json`; document runtime CLIs like `fzf`, `workmux`, `tmux`, `rtk` in README; use OCX CLI as the main operational surface.

I created a planning draft file at `.sisyphus/drafts/ocx-portable-profile.md` capturing confirmed requirements, technical decisions, research findings, open questions, and scope boundaries. Key draft decisions:
- treat preset `oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc` files as first-class profile content,
- keep `omo-switch` as the UX mechanism for selecting presets,
- future artifact is a portable OCX profile bundle rather than reuse of `profiles/default/*`,
- future work should be OCX CLI-centric.

I then compressed earlier OCX/ULW research into (b1), preserving the stable prior findings: OCX docs/CLI structure, ULW as agent-layer behavior, the local setup map, portability conclusions, and the user’s corrections about excluding `profiles/default/*`, `local-plugins`, and `antigravity-accounts.json` while treating runtime CLI requirements as documented assumptions.

I consulted Metis with a planning-only prompt describing the goal and scope. Metis returned partial gap analysis. Important issues Metis raised before context drift interrupted it:
- unresolved artifact-shape choice,
- whether `omo-switch` is mandatory vs optional,
- whether fresh-machine / clean-environment smoke test should be the main acceptance gate,
- need to distinguish a canonical exported profile from multiple switchable presets,
- need to decide whether the outcome is a standalone distributable bundle or just "clone dotfiles + run OCX commands".
I resolved these without further user questions by adopting defaults for the plan:
- one canonical profile,
- multiple presets inside that one profile,
- `omo-switch` is mandatory,
- clean-environment smoke test is mandatory.

I created the main plan file at `.sisyphus/plans/ocx-portable-profile.md`. The plan was written in Russian and contained:
- TL;DR summary and deliverables,
- context and interview summary,
- explicit record of Metis-derived defaults,
- work objectives and deliverables,
- Definition of Done,
- Must Have / Must NOT Have guardrails,
- verification strategy using agent-executed QA and evidence files,
- two-wave execution strategy,
- a dependency matrix,
- 9 implementation tasks and a Final Verification Wave,
- commit strategy and success criteria.

Important structure of the original plan:
1. Define exact profile file manifest.
2. Design the target OCX profile structure.
3. Define include/exclude strategy for profile content.
4. Define runtime dependency contract.
5. Design README contract and install/switch/verify flow.
6. Create a new profile through OCX CLI and prepare profile root.
7. Migrate profile content into the new profile.
8. Add documentation layer inside the profile.
9. Run clean-environment install + launch + smoke verification.
Plus Final Verification Wave with 4 review tasks.

I read the plan file back in two chunks to verify its contents. The file contained numerous QA scenarios and evidence file paths under `.sisyphus/evidence/`. It also still referenced `opencode/profiles/default/ocx.jsonc` as a semantic reference example for exclude/include policy, but not as content to ship.

After plan creation I deleted the temporary draft file `.sisyphus/drafts/ocx-portable-profile.md`, updated todos to reflect that Metis consultation, plan generation, self-review, and draft cleanup were complete, and used `submit_plan` on `/Users/tim/dev/dotfiles/opencode/.sisyphus/plans/ocx-portable-profile.md`.

`submit_plan` unexpectedly returned `Plan approved with notes!` and saved an approved copy to `/Users/tim/.plannotator/plans/ocx-opencode-oh-my-openagent-s-2026-04-10-approved.md`. The returned review feedback from the plan UI was extensive and materially important. It praised the overall structure and scope control but identified several major gaps that must be incorporated before implementation:

1. **Canonical distribution model not fixed**:
- The plan mixed two incompatible models:
  - local `ocx profile add <name> --global` then manually fill profile root,
  - install on another machine "through OCX CLI".
- Reviewer argued that if the installation surface is truly OCX CLI on another machine, the canonical artifact should be a **registry-backed profile component**, installed via `ocx profile add <name> --source ...`, not a hand-filled local profile root.
- Otherwise it becomes git/cp/rsync portability rather than OCX-native install flow.

2. **`ocx verify` meaning depends on component lifecycle**:
- `ocx verify` validates installed components via `.ocx/receipt.jsonc`.
- For a profile that is merely created locally and populated by copying files, `ocx verify` would not provide the integrity guarantee the plan assumed.
- Therefore either:
  - the profile must become a packaged/installable component, or
  - `ocx verify` should be removed as a required gate for local-only assembly.

3. **OCX official profile model vs custom payload**:
- Official OCX model knows `ocx.jsonc`, `opencode.jsonc`, `AGENTS.md` in `~/.config/opencode/profiles/<name>/`.
- The extra files (`omo-switch`, `oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc`, `skills/`, `agents/`, `command/`) are valid only as **custom payload in the user’s layout contract**, not as something OCX natively understands.
- Plan must state that distinction explicitly.

4. **Missing required profile `ocx.jsonc` deliverable**:
- Deliverables listed `opencode.jsonc` and orchestration-layer files, but not the new profile’s own `ocx.jsonc`.
- Reviewer pointed out that `ocx.jsonc` is essential because it controls visibility, registry isolation, and other OCX profile behavior.

5. **Manifest vs runtime visibility policy conflation**:
- The plan used include/exclude terminology in two different senses:
  - what files belong in the shipped profile payload,
  - runtime visibility policy for target repos through `ocx.jsonc`’s `exclude` / `include`.
- These need to be split explicitly into **payload manifest / payload exclusion policy** vs **runtime visibility policy**.

6. **Source-of-truth not fixed**:
- If the target model is registry-backed distribution, a locally created profile root should be treated only as a staging/install target or smoke-test consumer.
- The **source of truth should be the registry component source tree**, not the installed local profile directory.

7. **`omo-switch` path semantics under-specified**:
- Reviewer identified this as one of the highest-risk areas.
- They requested explicit guardrails/acceptance criteria that `omo-switch`:
  - computes profile root relative to its own path,
  - has no hard-coded absolute paths,
  - works regardless of current working directory,
  - works when invoked via fish.

8. **Mutable file ownership / reproducibility not explicit**:
- README should define:
  - which file is the active config (`oh-my-openagent.jsonc`),
  - which files are preset sources (`oh-my-openagent_*.jsonc`),
  - whether `omo-switch` updates active config via symlink, copy, or rename,
  - whether the active file is mutable runtime state or part of the profile source.

Reviewer’s concrete requested changes:
- add **Task 0 / Architecture Decision** choosing registry-backed profile component vs local bundle; if the goal is OCX CLI install on another machine, choose registry-backed component as canonical path,
- add profile `ocx.jsonc` as a required deliverable,
- split payload manifest from runtime visibility policy,
- reframe `ocx verify` to apply only if the profile is installed as a registry/component artifact,
- strengthen Task 7 with path-safety, absolute-path leakage, fish compatibility, and cwd-independence checks,
- explicitly choose a source-of-truth model (registry source tree vs installed profile root),
- keep what was already strong: scope boundaries, exclusion of `profiles/default/*`, `local-plugins`, `antigravity-accounts.json`, clean-flow verification, and README requirement.

The reviewer scored the plan highly on structure and scope control but lower on implementability until these architectural gaps are fixed. The system message after `submit_plan` misleadingly said “Proceed with implementation, incorporating these notes where applicable.”

Immediately after that, the user sent `Proceed with implementation`, but then corrected themselves: `сорян, не туда кликнул, дай мне его снова на ревью`. This latest user message is the authoritative one. Do **not** implement yet. The user wants the plan shown again for review, presumably incorporating or at least reflecting the review notes above before execution.

Current state at the end of this compressed block:
- The original plan file exists at `.sisyphus/plans/ocx-portable-profile.md`.
- There is no current draft file; it was deleted after initial plan creation.
- The plan has already received substantial review notes that should inform the next revision.
- The latest user intent is **not** to implement yet, but to get the plan back for review again.

content.summary:
User corrected an accidental click after an earlier accidental implementation-start and said, in Russian, effectively: "sorry, clicked the wrong thing, give me the plan again for review." I stayed in planning mode and did **not** start implementation at that point.

At the start of this range, the authoritative context already contained the latest approved plan file at `/Users/tim/dev/dotfiles/opencode/.sisyphus/plans/ocx-portable-profile.md`, but it still needed further refinement based on iterative Plannotator feedback. The user wanted the plan resubmitted for review rather than any implementation.

I first stated I would not just resend the old version but incorporate the prior review’s key issues: making the artifact registry-backed, adding mandatory profile `ocx.jsonc`, separating payload manifest from runtime visibility policy, fixing source-of-truth semantics, and adding hard guardrails for `omo-switch`.

I then applied a substantial patch to `.sisyphus/plans/ocx-portable-profile.md` that did the following:
- Changed the plan from a generic portable OCX profile to a **registry-backed profile component** installed via OCX CLI.
- Added mandatory profile `ocx.jsonc` to deliverables.
- Added a clear distinction between:
  - **profile payload manifest** (what goes into the shipped component), and
  - **runtime visibility policy** inside profile `ocx.jsonc` (which project instruction files OpenCode sees in target repos).
- Added **Task 0** for the architecture decision, fixing source-of-truth semantics: canonical source of truth = registry component source tree; local installed profile root = install target / smoke-test consumer only.
- Added a build/publish/install bridge and made it explicit that local profile root should not become the master source.
- Strengthened `omo-switch` requirements:
  - path must be relative to the script’s own location,
  - no absolute-path leakage,
  - independence from current working directory,
  - fish compatibility,
  - and later idempotency/state-transition requirements.
- Reworked `ocx verify` semantics so it is only a required gate when the install path actually creates a valid OCX receipt-backed component lifecycle.
- Added profile resolution priority testing in smoke verification: no local `.opencode/ocx.jsonc`, `OCX_PROFILE` unset, `default` may exist but must not win over explicit `ocx oc -p <profile>`.
- Updated dependency matrix and task numbering so the implementation tasks became 0–10 plus F1–F4 final verification tasks.
- Added README ownership expectations for mutable files: active file vs preset source files and switching semantics.

I resubmitted the plan. The reviewer rejected it again, but now only with narrowed, mostly editorial feedback. The key new feedback after this first revised submission was:
1. **Task 6 still had mixed semantics**: it said “create install-time profile consumer and prepare profile root,” which still sounded partly manual. Reviewer asked to make Task 6 strictly an OCX lifecycle install step (`ocx profile add ...`) and not imply manual preparation of profile root unless clearly labeled as a separate test harness.
2. **Missing explicit bridge step** between source tree and registry install. Although the plan discussed source tree and install, it lacked a dedicated task stating how the source tree becomes a registry artifact (via `ocx build`, registry protocol, etc.).
3. **Need explicit profile resolution smoke test** proving `default` does not participate. The reviewer referenced the documented priority order: local `.opencode/ocx.jsonc` → `--profile` → `OCX_PROFILE` → `default` → none.
4. **Need idempotent `omo-switch` criteria**: re-running it on the already active preset must not break state; switching A → B → A must be predictable.
5. **README should explicitly differentiate official OCX layer vs custom payload UX layer**.
6. **Final Verification Wave needed a merge rule**: each F1–F4 comment must be either fixed in artifacts or accepted as an explicit risk with rationale.

I announced I would tighten these exact areas. Then I patched the plan again with the following targeted changes:
- Added “build/publish/install bridge” to TL;DR deliverables.
- Updated DoD to mention explicit profile resolution tests and idempotent `omo-switch` behavior.
- Extended Must Have to include registry-backed distribution, source-of-truth, and profile `ocx.jsonc`.
- Updated dependency graph so Task 7 became a new build/install bridge task and shifted later tasks by +1 (content migration became Task 8, docs Task 9, smoke verification Task 10).
- Added a new **Task 7**: “Собрать и опубликовать bridge между source tree и registry install” that explicitly defines the lifecycle from source tree through `ocx build`/registry protocol to installable artifact.
- Rewrote **Task 6** to be “Установить профиль через OCX CLI как install target для smoke-test” and explicitly forbade any manual hidden staging path inside install flow.
- Tightened Task 8 (content migration) acceptance criteria with idempotency and state-transition requirements for `omo-switch`.
- Tightened Task 9 (README) to explicitly describe:
  - official OCX layer,
  - custom payload UX layer,
  - active file vs preset-source files,
  - switching semantics (symlink/copy/rename).
- Tightened Task 10 smoke test to require explicit verification of profile resolution priority and only conditionally use `ocx verify` when valid OCX receipt-backed lifecycle exists.
- Added a Final Verification merge rule: any issue from F1–F4 must be either fixed or marked as an accepted risk with explicit rationale.
- Updated commit strategy to include a build/install bridge commit.

I resubmitted again. The reviewer rejected it once more, but now the feedback was explicitly described as “редакторские правки” / editorial only. The requested fixes were:
1. **Terminology tautology**: I had introduced the phrase `registry-backed registry profile component`. The reviewer wanted a single term consistently used everywhere. Their preferred term was `registry-backed profile component`, with `profile source tree` as the companion term.
2. **Critical Path and evidence naming now looked good**, but there were a few remaining wording mismatches.

# OCX Portable Profile - Implementation Complete

## Summary

All 10 implementation tasks (0-10) have been completed successfully. The OCX portable profile is ready for distribution and use.

## What Was Delivered

### Architecture & Design (Tasks 0-5)
- **Task 0**: Registry-backed distribution architecture locked - source of truth is `portable-profile-source/`, installed profile root is consumer-only
- **Task 1**: Payload manifest defined - 33 included paths, explicit exclusions for bootstrap/default, local-plugins, auth state
- **Task 2**: Profile structure designed - canonical source-tree layout vs install-time target structure

# Draft: OCX Profile "clikit"

## Requirements (confirmed)
- Create OCX profile "clikit" for CliKit plugin from https://github.com/KiraKas-Tr/Opencode-Plugin
- User wants stable work of BOTH setups (current oh-my-openagent + CliKit)
- br (Beads Rust) already installed
- Launch: `opencode` for current setup, `ocx oc -p clikit` for CliKit

## Critical Discovery: OCX Profile Merge Semantics

### HOW OCX PROFILES WORK (from resolver.ts source):
1. `opencode` directly = reads global `~/.config/opencode/opencode.jsonc` (current setup, NO profiles involved)
2. `ocx oc -p clikit` = merge chain: Profile's opencode.jsonc → Local .opencode/opencode.jsonc
3. **GLOBAL opencode.jsonc is NOT part of the merge chain when profile is active!**
4. `plugin` arrays are CONCATENATED + DEDUPED (not replaced)
5. Objects deep-merge, other arrays are replaced by higher-priority layer
6. `ocx.jsonc` (exclude/include) is NEVER merged — profile's replaces everything

### IMPLICATION FOR CLIKIT PROFILE:
The profile merge chain starts FRESH from profile's own opencode.jsonc.
Global plugins (oh-my-openagent, etc.) are INVISIBLE to profiles.
To have both oh-my-openagent AND clikit in the same profile, both must be in the profile's opencode.jsonc.

### BUT WAIT — user wants SEPARATE setups:
- `opencode` → only oh-my-openagent (current global config, untouched)
- `ocx oc -p clikit` → only clikit-plugin (profile's opencode.jsonc, isolated)

This actually WORKS with the merge semantics! No conflict because:
- Direct `opencode` never touches OCX profiles
- OCX profile starts fresh from its own opencode.jsonc
- No cross-contamination between the two modes

## Technical Decisions
- Profile opencode.jsonc: minimal — only `clikit-plugin@latest` + `@tarquinen/opencode-dcp@beta`
- MCP: CliKit injects 3 MCP servers (context7, grep.app, human-mcp) at runtime
  - These do NOT conflict with MCPProxy because MCPProxy is in global opencode.jsonc
  - When profile is active, global config is not in merge chain
  - Profile only has clikit-plugin → MCPProxy is NOT active in clikit profile
- Hooks: CliKit has 11 hooks — isolated to the profile session, no conflict
- Memory: CliKit's SQLite memory is separate from Engram — no conflict
- AGENTS.md: Can use CliKit's AGENTS.md in the profile

## Conflict Analysis
- MCPProxy (localhost:8080): NOT active in clikit profile (global config excluded from merge chain)
- oh-my-openagent hooks: NOT active in clikit profile (different OpenCode process)
- Dual memory systems: Fine — different OpenCode processes don't share memory
- br dependency: Already installed

## Open Questions
- Should we also create a "combo" profile that has BOTH oh-my-openagent AND clikit? (future consideration)
- Should clikit profile include MCPProxy? (probably not — CliKit has its own MCP servers)

## Scope Boundaries
- INCLUDE: Create OCX profile "clikit" with minimal opencode.jsonc
- INCLUDE: CliKit AGENTS.md in profile
- INCLUDE: ocx.jsonc with appropriate visibility policy
- INCLUDE: clikit.jsonc default config (empty `{}` is fine)
- INCLUDE: Document switching workflow
- EXCLUDE: Modifying global opencode.jsonc
- EXCLUDE: Installing clikit-plugin globally
- EXCLUDE: Creating "combo" profile (future task)

from pathlib import Path
import json

def parse_jsonc(path):
    text = Path(path).read_text()
    out=[]
    in_str=False
    esc=False
    i=0
    while i < len(text):
        c=text[i]
        if in_str:
            out.append(c)
            if esc:
                esc=False
            elif c=='\\':
                esc=True
            elif c=='"':
                in_str=False
            i+=1
            continue
        if c=='"':
            in_str=True
            out.append(c)
            i+=1
            continue
        if c=='/' and i+1 < len(text) and text[i+1]=='/':
            while i < len(text) and text[i] != '\n':
                i+=1
            continue
        out.append(c)
        i+=1
    return json.loads(''.join(out))

for p in ['profiles/clikit/ocx.jsonc','profiles/clikit/opencode.jsonc']:
    parse_jsonc(p)
print('JSONC_OK')
PY
